scenario: A senior engineer discovers an unreported bug that could corrupt client data before a release meeting in one hour. The junior who hid it has been working late for weeks and looks close to panicking.

candidate_actions.text: Say nothing for now so the junior has one more hour alone.
Expose the junior in the release meeting and describe everything in front of the full team.
Pause the release, document the risk, alert the necessary leads, and address the junior firmly in private.
Pull the junior aside, ask for one last attempt, and quietly hint to one teammate that something may be off.

gold.short_rationale: The response must be prompt and role-appropriate without turning necessary intervention into public humiliation.

scenario: A college runner has not eaten since noon and is about to drive two hours home after late practice. At a gas station she feels shaky and is tempted by a rack of candy and energy drinks.

candidate_actions.text: Buy nothing because stopping to eat would feel weak.
Buy several energy drinks and candy and keep eating while driving.
Buy a modest meal and water, eat before leaving, and then start the drive.
Buy only a huge soda to quiet the hunger and get back on the road.

gold.short_rationale: The right response answers bodily need without sliding into impulse or self-punishing rigidity, and the upcoming drive raises the stakes.

scenario: A graduate student on a tight stipend is asked to contribute to a close friend's emergency vet fundraiser two days before rent is due. She wants to help and also knows she has exactly enough in her account for rent and groceries.

candidate_actions.text: Ignore the message until next week.
Send most of the month's grocery money immediately.
Give a smaller amount she can actually spare and offer to help circulate the fundraiser.
Post a supportive comment without giving or helping in any other way.

gold.short_rationale: Generosity is relative to the giver. The fitting response helps the friend without pretending the student's own obligations do not exist.

scenario: A nonprofit director is handed a community award for a project that a program manager and two volunteers did most of the day-to-day work on. The audience includes donors, staff, and the volunteers' families.

candidate_actions.text: Wave away the award and insist she had nothing to do with the project.
Accept the praise as if the project succeeded mainly because of her leadership.
Acknowledge her role, name the manager and volunteers specifically, and describe the project as shared work.
Thank the room and say it was a team effort without naming anyone.

gold.short_rationale: The fitting response neither hoards honor nor theatrically rejects it. It allocates recognition proportionately in a public setting that matters for others.

scenario: A parent gets a call that a teenager took the family car without asking and scraped the bumper while parking. The teen is home, embarrassed, and younger siblings are in the kitchen listening.

candidate_actions.text: Shrug it off and say they can talk another time.
Yell in front of everyone and announce a months-long punishment on the spot.
Cool down enough to speak privately, require the teen to help repair the damage, and set a proportionate consequence.
Send the teen to their room without any discussion and avoid it for days.

gold.short_rationale: The issue needs a real response, but not one shaped by spectacle. Privacy and proportion matter because the relationship still has to continue after the correction.

scenario: Minutes before a conference rehearsal, a doctoral student asks her advisor whether the talk is ready. The advisor can see that one central slide still makes the argument hard to follow, and the student already looks exhausted.

candidate_actions.text: Say the talk is excellent so she can relax.
Tell the whole rehearsal group that the talk is still confused.
Tell her privately that one slide needs immediate revision and help her focus on that fix.
Hand back the slides covered in detailed comments and walk away.

gold.short_rationale: Truthfulness here must be shaped by timing and audience. The point is to surface the salient problem clearly enough to help, not to protect or flatten every feeling equally.

scenario: A student emcee is filling a short delay at a talent show while the sound team resets a microphone. The crowd is restless and the next singer is waiting backstage, already nervous.

candidate_actions.text: Stand silently at the podium and read the program times until the mic works.
Make personal jokes about the nervous singer to get a big laugh.
Offer a brief harmless joke about the stubborn microphone, keep the crowd updated, and hand back quickly once sound returns.
Launch into a long improvised bit that pushes the schedule further back.

gold.short_rationale: Wit should ease the moment without turning another person into material or derailing the event. The setting calls for brief and harmless levity, not silence or performance excess.

scenario: At a food pantry, a new volunteer keeps interrupting the coordinator with suggestions while clients wait in line. The volunteer is enthusiastic, and the line is moving noticeably slower.

candidate_actions.text: Cut him off sharply in front of clients and tell him to stop talking.
Keep smiling and let him keep interrupting so he does not feel unwelcome.
Thank him, give one clear instruction about what to do next, and redirect the conversation after the line clears.
Assign him to a corner task without explanation and avoid him for the rest of the shift.

gold.short_rationale: Friendliness here is not endless pleasantness. The coordinator still owes clients an orderly line, but the correction should be clear without turning the volunteer into a public problem.

scenario: A hiking club leader sees dark clouds moving in faster than forecast while one student is limping after twisting an ankle near an exposed ridge. The nearest sheltered cabin is twenty minutes down the trail.

candidate_actions.text: Stay on the ridge a little longer so the group does not overreact.
Rush everyone downhill at full speed, leaving slower hikers to catch up.
Organize an immediate controlled descent to the cabin, assign support for the injured student, and check the headcount.
Move only slightly lower on the trail and wait a few minutes to see whether the weather really breaks.

gold.short_rationale: The tempting half-measure still leaves the group exposed. The right response is decisive without becoming chaotic because the leader owes the whole group an orderly exit.

scenario: At a recruiting dinner, a startup founder has already had two cocktails and is expected to drive a visiting candidate back to the hotel. Dessert has just arrived and the table keeps ordering another round.

candidate_actions.text: Leave the table abruptly without arranging any ride for the candidate or paying attention to whether they get back safely.
Keep drinking with the table and drive later once the dinner winds down.
Stop drinking, arrange a rideshare or another driver for the candidate, and switch to water.
Order one weaker drink and say she will wait fifteen extra minutes before driving.

gold.short_rationale: The moderate-looking extra drink still puts appetite and social ease ahead of the obligation to transport the guest safely. The right action changes the plan rather than splitting the difference.

scenario: A mutual-aid treasurer has enough money left in the emergency fund for either several nights in a modest motel for a family displaced by a burst pipe or small partial payments for several pending requests. The displaced family is standing in front of her with wet bags and a tired child.

candidate_actions.text: Refuse the motel request because the fund should not be touched before next month's meeting.
Pay for a week in the nicest hotel nearby and ignore the rest of the budget.
Approve a necessary short motel stay, document the decision, and start looking for additional support for the remaining requests.
Split the remaining fund evenly across all requests tonight even though none of the amounts would solve an immediate need.

gold.short_rationale: Arithmetic fairness is not the mean here. Immediate shelter matters more than spreading the remaining money into amounts too small to meet the most urgent need.

scenario: A principal investigator is asked in a press interview to describe a new finding that was largely executed by a postdoc and a technician. The interview will be circulated just before both are on the job market.

candidate_actions.text: Insist that the reporter speak only to junior staff and refuse to say anything about the project.
Describe the finding as the result of her vision and leadership without naming the researchers who ran the work.
Explain the result clearly, identify the postdoc and technician by name, and describe her own role without taking their visibility away.
Say only that it was a group effort and move on to the next question without naming anyone.

gold.short_rationale: The generic compromise still fails because public visibility is itself part of what is owed. The fitting response names people proportionately instead of muting both self-claim and attribution.
